result:
Thereis a peculiar comfort in the concept of deep time. When I stand outside on a clear night and look up at the stars, I am not just looking at space; I am looking at history. The light reaching my eyes left its source long before I took my first breath, and in some cases, long before our planet even formed. We are constantly surrounded by ghosts of the ancient past, shining brilliantly in the dark.

It makes our human anxieties feel wonderfully small. We spend our days worrying about deadlines, emails, and social missteps, operating under the illusion that these fleeting moments carry the immense weight of the universe. Yet, against the backdrop of cosmic timelines, where millions of years are reduced to mere blips, our worries lose their sharp edges. This isn't a depressing thought; rather, it is a profound relief. The universe does not care about my mistakes, and there is a terrible, beautiful freedom in that indifference.

We are granted a brief window of consciousness, a tiny sliver of time in which to experience the warmth of the sun, the bitter taste of coffee, and the sound of rain against glass. Deep time teaches us not to squander this sliver in endless worry, but to marvel at the absurdity of our existence. We are the universe looking back at itself, a momentary spark of awareness in an otherwise sleeping cosmos. And for this one incredibly brief, brilliant flash of conscious existence, we are enough.

We are the universe looking back at itself, a momentary spark of awareness in an otherwise sleeping cosmos. And for this one incredibly brief, brilliant flash of conscious existence, we are enough.

    We are granted a brief window of consciousness, a sliver of time in which to experience the warmth of the sun, the taste of coffee, and the sound of rain against glass.     There is a peculiar comfort in the concept of deep time. When I stand outside on a clear night and look up at the stars, I am not just looking at space; I am looking at history. The light reaching my eyes left its source long before I took my first breath, and in some cases, long before our planet even formed. We are constantly surrounded by ghosts of the ancient past, shining brilliantly in the dark.

    It makes our human anxieties feel wonderfully small. We spend our days worrying about deadlines, emails, and social missteps, operating under the illusion that these fleeting moments carry the immense weight of the universe. Yet, against the backdrop of cosmic timelines—where millions of years are reduced to mere blips—our worries lose their sharp edges. This isn't a depressing thought; rather, it is a profound relief. The universe does not care about my mistakes, and there is a terrible, beautiful freedom in that indifference.

    We are granted a brief window of consciousness, a tiny sliver of time in which to experience the warmth of the sun, the bitter taste of coffee, and the sound of rain against glass. Deep time teaches us not to squander this sliver in endless worry, but to marvel at the absurdity of our existence. We are the universe looking back at itself, a momentary spark of awareness in an otherwise sleeping cosmos. For this brief flash, we are enough.
